summary: My grandfather came from England about 125 years ago before making his way to the United States. Though I was born in Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin, my family's roots trace back to England through him. England represents the origin of my paternal lineage and the starting point of our family's journey to America.

On his way from England to the United States, my grandfather stopped in Jamaica for some time. It was there that he met my grandmother, who was of Spanish and French descent. Jamaica was a significant place for my family because it was where my grandparents' paths crossed before settling in New Orleans.

My family made their home in the United States after my grandfather's journey from England and Jamaica. We settled in New Orleans initially, where my grandfather was involved in buying and selling cotton. Later, my father practiced medicine and served as an Episcopal minister in places like Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin. The United States is where my family established roots and where I was born and educated.

New Orleans was where my grandparents settled after meeting in Jamaica. My grandfather engaged in the cotton trade there, buying and selling cotton. My father was born and educated in New Orleans and was baptized in the Roman Catholic Church there. It was a foundational place for my family's early American history.

My father was baptized in the Roman Catholic Church, which suggests my grandmother was likely a member since my grandfather was a Freemason and probably not affiliated with that denomination. The church played a role in my family's religious background, at least on my grandmother's side. This connection is part of the religious heritage passed down through my family.

My grandfather was a Freemason, which distinguished his religious and social affiliations from my grandmother's Roman Catholic background. This membership likely influenced his community standing and personal beliefs. Freemasonry was an important part of his identity during his life in New Orleans and beyond.

I was born in Prairie du Chien, one of the oldest towns in Wisconsin, known for its fort built during the Indian Wars. My father practiced medicine and surgery there after leaving the ministry. It was also where my mother moved as a child and where I lost both my father and sister to diphtheria when I was very young. Prairie du Chien holds a deep personal and family significance for me.

Prairie du Chien, where I was born, was the site of a fort built during the Indian Wars. This historical context marks the town as an important military and frontier location in Wisconsin's past. Though I did not experience the wars myself, their legacy was part of the environment I grew up in.

My father gained a lot of surgical experience while serving in Jackson's army. This military service contributed to his medical expertise, which he later applied in his civilian practice in Prairie du Chien. Jackson's army was thus a formative part of my father's professional background.

My mother was born in Michigan and was of German and English descent. She moved to Prairie du Chien as a small girl, where she later married my father. Michigan represents my maternal family's origins before settling in Wisconsin.

After my stepfather sold his farm around 1900, we moved to West Salem, a small town near La Crosse. I attended high school there and spent much time reading outside of school. West Salem was an important place for my adolescent education and personal development.

West Salem, where I went to high school, is near La Crosse, Wisconsin. Though I did not live directly in La Crosse, its proximity was part of my regional environment during my youth. La Crosse represents the broader area of my upbringing in western Wisconsin.

During my high school years in West Salem, I read extensively, including the works of Mark Twain. His literature was among the standard American authors I enjoyed alongside Kipling and European philosophers. Twain's writings were part of the foundation of my literary education.

Rudyard Kipling was one of the authors whose works I read thoroughly during my youth in West Salem. Along with Mark Twain and European philosophers, Kipling's stories influenced my early intellectual development. His writing was a significant part of my literary exploration.

I studied many European philosophers, including Hegel, whose work I found quite challenging. It took me years to understand that even Hegel himself might not have fully grasped all he wrote. His philosophy was a tough but important part of my intellectual growth during my formative years.

Bernarr Macfadden's magazine was a valuable resource for me; I bought the second issue and rarely missed a copy for years. Although many contributors were eccentric, the magazine contained sound information, especially on health topics like sunshine and vitamins. Macfadden's work preceded and influenced later efforts like those of Collier's in fighting patent medicine frauds.

While Bernarr Macfadden began the fight against patent medicine frauds, Collier's magazine is usually credited with this work. I was aware of this connection and recognized Macfadden's earlier contributions. Collier's represents a continuation of important health reform efforts I followed.

In 1905, I attended the University of Wisconsin–Madison, where I majored in mathematics for my first degree. Although I was not very fond of mathematics, I later taught some elementary college math courses. I also earned a degree in physics there, which suited me better and shaped my academic career.

My first paying job after earning my bachelor's degree was as an assistant instructor in the physics department at Louisiana State University in Baton Rouge. I earned $800 a year and faced a heavy teaching load, especially since the department was understaffed. The head of the department was more interested in his sugar cane plantation than teaching, which made the year challenging.

After leaving Louisiana State University, I took a research assistantship at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. There, I worked on photo-electric cells to improve their sensitivity for astronomical measurements, such as studying variable stars. This position marked an important step in my scientific career.

The head of the physics department at Louisiana State University, who later became university president, was fond of quoting Tennyson: 'Knowledge crimes but wisdom lingers.' Despite his limited work ethic, he was an effective leader who knew how to direct others. This quotation stuck with me as a reflection on knowledge and wisdom.

I served six years in the Philippine Islands as a teacher and later as an assistant professor of physics at the University of Manila. During this time, I traveled extensively, including trips to Japan, China, and through the mountains of Luzon. The Philippines was a significant chapter in my life, both professionally and culturally.

In the Philippines, I was assigned to northern Luzon, where I first encountered the Filipino people. I also made a challenging trip on foot through the mountainous region inhabited by the Ilongot people, known as the wild people's country. Luzon was a place of both teaching and adventurous exploration for me.

While serving in the Philippines, I made trips to Japan, which broadened my understanding of the region. These travels were part of my exposure to different cultures during my six years in Asia. Japan was one of the important neighboring countries I visited during that period.

During my time in the Philippines, I also traveled to China, further expanding my experience in East Asia. These trips complemented my academic and cultural experiences while working in the Philippines. China was part of the broader regional context of my service abroad.

I encountered the Ilongot people during a difficult mountain trek in central Luzon. They are a small, sturdy group known for their extensive mountain terraces and unique customs, including dog feasting. Although they spoke little English or Spanish, I observed their way of life and was impressed by their physical endurance and cultural practices.
The transcript does not provide specific personal experiences or detailed information about the Moros from my perspective, so I cannot offer a direct summary of my relationship with them.

While in the Philippines, I was aware of the presence of Islam, particularly among groups like the Moros. However, my personal interactions or detailed experiences related to Islam were limited, so I cannot speak extensively about it from my own perspective.

entity: Roman Catholic Church in the Philippines
summary: During my time in the Philippines, the Roman Catholic Church was a prominent institution, especially in Manila where I taught. Although I did not describe direct involvement with the church there, its influence was evident in the cultural and religious landscape of the islands. This church was part of the colonial and social context I experienced.

I served as an assistant professor of physics at the University of Manila for four years. Manila was my base during much of my time in the Philippines and a center for my academic work. The city also provided opportunities for travel and cultural exposure in the region.
